text: The surgeon begins dissection laterally in Bogros' space, progressing medially towards the supravesical space, creating a peritoneal flap.

The surgeon dissects past the medial umbilical ligament, extending to the contralateral medial umbilical ligament, and continues into the supravesicular space. The dissection crosses into the peritoneal flap, progressing towards Bagros' space.
The surgeon pushes the fat laterally, maintaining a thin dissection on the peritoneal flap to cover the nerves running along the lateral side wall.
The surgical field shows a standard direct inguinal hernia without visible evidence of recurrence in the direct space.
The surgeon encounters some adhesions in the indirect space at the deep internal ring, with no visible mesh present.
The surgeon dissects medially towards the contralateral medial umbilical ligament, creating space past the symphysis pubis in the direct inguinal region.
The surgeon manipulates tissue to bring the antisac into view, using gentle pushing and occasional cautery to separate and sweep away surrounding tissue.
The surgeon adjusts the camera to an oblique angle and applies cautery while gently pushing tissue aside.
The surgeon maintains close proximity to Cooper's ligament and the symphysis pubis, ensuring precise dissection along these anatomical landmarks.
The surgeon extends the dissection far enough inferiorly to remove the obturator plug, visually checking for the presence of an obturator hernia.
The surgical field shows no direct hernia, no recurrent hernia sac, and no cord lipoma visible in the explored area.
The surgeon positions the mesh laterally to ensure it lays flat and does not roll during the closure of the flap.

The surgeon fixates the mesh using two-ovicral sutures, attaching it twice to Cooper's ligament. The fixation includes a high lateral and medial placement, positioned just medial to the direct hernia sac.
The surgeon performs a lateral fixation of the mesh, ensuring it is not overly tightened to avoid wrinkling, which could potentially cause more pain.
The surgeon secures the mesh medially on Cooper's ligament, just medial to the iliac vein, to prevent mesh migration.
The surgeon carefully dissects close to the iliac vein, moving towards the symphysis pubis in the middle, ensuring no recurrence or tissue protrusion occurs in this area.

The surgeon achieves triangular fixation of the direct inguinal hernia space, focusing on the high medial portion. The fixation is visible on the right side, while the stitch on the bottom left is not visible. The mesh is cinched up to ensure it remains flat.
The surgeon performs a standard peritoneal flap closure using two OV lock sutures, securing the flap in place.
